subject: Chemistry
q: An analyst wants to convert 1 L HCl of pH=1 to a solution of HCl of pH=2. The volume of water needed to do this dilution is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)
question: An analyst wants to convert 1 L HCl of pH=1 to a solution of HCl of pH=2. The volume of water needed to do this dilution is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The concentration of H+ or say HCl in both the given solution is 10–1 and 10–2 mole L–1 respectively.

For the given amount of HCl the concentration depends over dilution hence

M1V1=M2V2M1M2=V2V1

10-110-2=V21L

V2=10 L=10000 mL

∆V=10000-1000 mL=9000 mL

subject: Chemistry
q:
In a sautrated solution of the sparingly soluble strong electrolyte $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ (Molecular mass $=283$) the equilibrium which sets in is
$\mathrm{AgIO}_{3(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{IO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
If the solubility product constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ at a given temperature is $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$, what is the mass of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ contained in $100 \mathrm{~ml}$ of its saturated solution?
question:
In a sautrated solution of the sparingly soluble strong electrolyte $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ (Molecular mass $=283$) the equilibrium which sets in is
$\mathrm{AgIO}_{3(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{IO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
If the solubility product constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ at a given temperature is $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$, what is the mass of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ contained in $100 \mathrm{~ml}$ of its saturated solution?
solution:
$\mathrm{AgIO}_3(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
Let the solubility of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ be $\mathrm{s}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}\right]$
$1.0 \times 10^{-8}=\mathrm{s}^2$
$\mathrm{s}=10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{litre}$
$=\frac{10^{-4} \times 283}{1000} \times 100$
$=283 \times 10^{-5}$
$=2.83 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} / 100 \mathrm{~ml}$
Hence, (B) is correct.

q: The solubility in water of a sparingly soluble salt $\mathrm{AB}$ is $1.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. Its solubility product number will be
question: The solubility in water of a sparingly soluble salt $\mathrm{AB}$ is $1.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. Its solubility product number will be
solution: $\begin{array}{ll}{[\mathrm{A}]=1.0 \times 10^{-5},} & {[\mathrm{~B}]=\left[1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right]} \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=[2 . \mathrm{B}]^2[\mathrm{~A}] \quad=\left[2 \times 10^{-5}\right]^2\left[1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right]=4 \times 10^{-15}\end{array}$

q: Sulphurous acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>588</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round off to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
question: Sulphurous acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>588</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round off to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
solution:
H2SO3 [Dibasic acid]

c=0.588 M

⇒ pH of solution is due to First dissociation only since Ka1>>Ka2

⇒ First dissociation of H2SO3

H2SO3(aq)⇌H⊕(aq)+HSO3-(aq) : ka1=1.7×10-2

t=      0

t       C-x              x                x

⇒Ka1=1.7100=H⊕HSO3-H2SO3

⇒1.7100=x20.588-x

⇒1.7×0.588-1.7x=100x2

⇒100x2+1.7x-1=0

⇒H⊕=x=-1.7+(1.7)2+4×100×12×100=0.09186

Therefore pH of sol. is :

pH=-logH⊕

⇒pH=-log0.09186=1.036≃1

Ksp=S(0.1)2

2×10-20=s×10-2⇒s=2×10-18

=x×10-18

x=2

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
solution:
Given at $330 \mathrm{~K}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{K}_w & =10^{-13.6} \\
\text { i.e. } \quad \mathrm{pK}_w & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\because \quad \mathrm{pOH}^2 & =-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\mathrm{pOH} & =-\log 10^{-4} \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+4 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} & =13.6-4 \\
& =9.6
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The solubility (in mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ $\left(K_{\mathrm{sp}}=1.0 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ in a $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution will be
question: The solubility (in mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ $\left(K_{\mathrm{sp}}=1.0 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ in a $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution will be
solution:
Let solubility of $\mathrm{AgCl}=x \mathrm{~mole} / \mathrm{L}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{AgCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& \text {i.e., } K_{\mathrm{sp}(\mathrm{AgCl})}=x \times x \\
& \mathrm{KCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right] \text {from } \mathrm{KCl}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}} \\
& \text { Total }\left[\mathrm{Cl}{ }^{-}\right] \text {in solution }=x+0.1 \\
& K_{s p}(\mathrm{AgCl})=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]=x(x+0.1) \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=x(x+0.1) \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=x^2+0.1 x \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=0.1 x \quad\left(\text { as } x^2< < 1\right) \\
& x=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider a weak base ' B ' of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5.699$. ' $x$ ' mL of 0.02 M HCl and ' y ' mL of 0.02 M weak base ' B ' are mixed to make 100 mL of a buffer of pH 9 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The values of ' $x$ ' and ' $y$ ' respectively are: 
[Given: $\log 2=0.3010, \log 3=0.4771, \log 5=0.699$]
question:
Consider a weak base ' B ' of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5.699$. ' $x$ ' mL of 0.02 M HCl and ' y ' mL of 0.02 M weak base ' B ' are mixed to make 100 mL of a buffer of pH 9 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The values of ' $x$ ' and ' $y$ ' respectively are: 
[Given: $\log 2=0.3010, \log 3=0.4771, \log 5=0.699$]
solution:
$pOH = pK_b + \log \left[ \dfrac{Salt}{Base} \right]$
$5 = 5.699 + \log \left[ \dfrac{Salt}{Base} \right]$
$\dfrac{x}{y - x} = \dfrac{1}{5}$
$6x = y$
$7x = 100$
$x = \dfrac{100}{7} \text{ ml}$
$y = \dfrac{600}{7} \text{ ml}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Solubility of calcium phosphate (molecular mass, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>) in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></msub></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Its solubility product at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be approximately.
question: Solubility of calcium phosphate (molecular mass, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>) in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></msub></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Its solubility product at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be approximately.
solution:
Given the solubility of Ca3PO42 is Wg/100 mL. The solubility in mol/L is S=WM×1000100mol/L.

The equilibrium reaction of calcium phosphate is 

Ca3PO42⇌3Ca2++2PO43-At equilibrium3S2S

Now, Ksp=Ca2+3PO43-2

⇒Ksp=3S32S2=108×S5

⇒Ksp=108×WM×105⇒Ksp=108×105×WM5

=1.08×107WM5

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing $5$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4OH$ solution with $250$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4Cl$ solution is _____ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
Given: $pK_b(NH_4OH) = 4.74$
$\log 2 = 0.30$
$\log 3 = 0.48$
$\log 5 = 0.70$
question:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing $5$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4OH$ solution with $250$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4Cl$ solution is _____ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
Given: $pK_b(NH_4OH) = 4.74$
$\log 2 = 0.30$
$\log 3 = 0.48$
$\log 5 = 0.70$
solution:
The given mixture forms a basic buffer solution.

Millimoles of base $NH_4OH = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ mmol

Millimoles of salt $NH_4Cl = 250 \times 0.1 = 25$ mmol

Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a basic buffer:

$pOH = pK_b + \log \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$

$pOH = 4.74 + \log \left( \dfrac{25}{0.5} \right)$

$pOH = 4.74 + \log (50)$

We know that $\log (50) = \log (5 \times 10) = \log 5 + \log 10 = 0.70 + 1 = 1.70$

$pOH = 4.74 + 1.70 = 6.44$

The pH of the solution is given by:

$pH = 14 - pOH$

$pH = 14 - 6.44 = 7.56$

$pH = 756 \times 10^{-2}$

Answer: $756$

subject: Chemistry
q:
600mL of 0.01M HCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01M H2SO4. The pH of the mixture is _____×10-2. (Nearest integer)

[Given log2=0.30,   log3=0.48,  log5=0.69,   log7=0.84,   log11=1.04]
question:
600mL of 0.01M HCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01M H2SO4. The pH of the mixture is _____×10-2. (Nearest integer)

[Given log2=0.30,   log3=0.48,  log5=0.69,   log7=0.84,   log11=1.04]
solution:
The number of millimoles = Molarity ×  Volume in mL

HCl produce one and H2SO4 produce two H+ ions respectively.

Total milimoles of H+=600×0.01+400×0.01×2=14
Thus, moles of hydrogen ion:
⇒H+=141000=14×10-3
⇒pH = -log[H+]=-log[14 × 10-3]

⇒pH=3-log14

=1.86

=186×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: Zirconium phosphate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><msub><mi>Zr</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></math> dissociates into three zirconium cations of charge​ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and four phosphate anions of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. If molar solubility of zirconium phosphate is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and its solubility product by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> then which of the following relationship between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is correct ​?
question: Zirconium phosphate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><msub><mi>Zr</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></math> dissociates into three zirconium cations of charge​ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and four phosphate anions of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. If molar solubility of zirconium phosphate is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and its solubility product by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> then which of the following relationship between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is correct ​?
options: <math><mrow>  <mtext>S</mtext>  <mo>=</mo>      <mrow>      <mfenced open="{" close="}" separators=",">        <mrow>          <msub>                  <mtext>K</mtext>                <mtext>sp</mtext>          </msub>          <mo>/</mo>          <msup>                <mrow>                  <mfenced open="(" close=")" separators=",">                    <mrow>                      <mn>6</mn>                      <mn>9</mn>                      <mn>1</mn>                      <mn>2</mn>                    </mrow>                  </mfenced>                </mrow>              <mrow>                <mn>1</mn>                <mo>/</mo>                <mn>7</mn>              </mrow>          </msup>        </mrow>      </mfenced>    </mrow>  </mrow></math>
solution:
In the saturated solution of

Zr 3 PO 4 4 + aq ⇌ 3 Zr 4 + 3 S molar + 4 PO 4 3 - 4 S molar

K sp = Zr 4 + 3 PO 4 3 - 4 = 3 S 3 4 S 4

= 2 7 × 2 5 6 × s 7 = 6 9 1 2   S 7

S = K sp 6 9 1 2 1 / 7

subject: Chemistry
q: The molar solubility(s) of zirconium phosphate with molecular formula $\left(\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}\right)_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right)_4$ is given by relation :
question: The molar solubility(s) of zirconium phosphate with molecular formula $\left(\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}\right)_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right)_4$ is given by relation :
solution:
$\begin{array}{cc}\mathrm{Zr}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_4(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons & 3 \mathrm{Zr}^{+4}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-3}(\mathrm{aq}) \\ - & 3 \mathrm{~s} \quad 4 \mathrm{~s}\end{array}$



$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}=(3 \mathrm{~s})^3(4 \mathrm{~s})^4=6912 \mathrm{~s}^7 \\ & \mathrm{~s}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right)^{1 / 7}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The first and second ionization constants of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ are $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. The concentration of $\mathrm{X}^{2-}$ in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ solution is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-15} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest Integer)
question: The first and second ionization constants of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ are $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. The concentration of $\mathrm{X}^{2-}$ in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ solution is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-15} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest Integer)
solution:
For a weak diprotic acid $H_2X$, the ionization steps are:
$H_2X \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HX^-$ with $K_{a1} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$
$HX^- \rightleftharpoons H^+ + X^{2-}$ with $K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$
Since $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$, the concentration of $H^+$ is primarily determined by the first ionization.
$[H^+] \approx \sqrt{K_{a1} \cdot C} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.1} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{-5} M$
Also, from the first ionization, $[HX^-] \approx [H^+] = 5 \times 10^{-5} M$
For the second ionization:
$K_{a2} = \frac{[H^+][X^{2-}]}{[HX^-]}$
Substituting the values: $1.0 \times 10^{-13} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})[X^{2-}]}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$
Thus, $[X^{2-}] = K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13} M$
To express this in the form $x \times 10^{-15} M$:
$1.0 \times 10^{-13} = 100 \times 10^{-15} M$
The value is 100.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given is a concentrated solution of a weak electrolyte $A_xB_y$ of concentration 'c' and dissociation constant 'K'. The degree of dissociation is given by :
question: Given is a concentrated solution of a weak electrolyte $A_xB_y$ of concentration 'c' and dissociation constant 'K'. The degree of dissociation is given by :
solution:
The dissociation reaction of the weak electrolyte $A_xB_y$ is:

$A_xB_y \rightleftharpoons xA^{y+} + yB^{x-}$

Initial concentrations:
$c \quad 0 \quad 0$

Equilibrium concentrations:
$c(1-\alpha) \quad cx\alpha \quad cy\alpha$

The dissociation constant $K$ is given by:

$K = \dfrac{[A^{y+}]^x [B^{x-}]^y}{[A_xB_y]}$

$K = \dfrac{(cx\alpha)^x (cy\alpha)^y}{c(1-\alpha)}$

$K = \dfrac{c^{x+y} x^x y^y \alpha^{x+y}}{c(1-\alpha)}$

$K = \dfrac{c^{x+y-1} x^x y^y \alpha^{x+y}}{1-\alpha}$

For a weak electrolyte, the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ is very small, so $1-\alpha \approx 1$.

$K = c^{x+y-1} x^x y^y \alpha^{x+y}$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$\alpha^{x+y} = \dfrac{K}{c^{x+y-1} x^x y^y}$

$\alpha = \left(\dfrac{K}{c^{x+y-1} x^x y^y}\right)^{\dfrac{1}{x+y}}$

Answer: $\left(\dfrac{K}{c^{x+y-1} x^x y^y}\right)^{\frac{1}{x+y}}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
question:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
solution:
For the insoluble salt barium sulphate, sulphate ion added is the common ion. Due to the common ion effect the solubility of barium sulphate decreases. Let us assume solubility of barium sulphate is S.

BaSO4s⇌Ba2+Saq+SO42-S+0.1aq
 K2SO40.1-0.1⟶ K+0.1aq+SO42-0.1aq
KSP=Ba2+SO42-
1×10-10=S(S+0.1)
≃0.1 S
S=10-9M=233×10-9gL-1

question: The minimum volume of water required to dissolve 0.1 g lead (II) chloride to get a saturated solution ( <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>s</mi><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup></math> ;atomic mass of <math><mi>P</mi><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>207</mn><mi> </mi><mi>u</mi></math> ) is:
solution:
(Ksp)PbCl2=32×10-9

PbCl2=Pb2+  s   +2Cl- 2s

Ksp= Pb2+ 2Cl-2

Ksp=4s3 = 32×10-9

s3 = 8×10-9

s=2×10-3M

wM.w×1VL=2×10-3

0.1278×1VL=2×10-3

VL=0.1×1000278×2=0.18 L

subject: Chemistry
q:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
question:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
options: $\mathrm{pH}$ of solution will be equal to $\mathrm{pH}$ of acetic acid
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH} & =\mathrm{pK}_a+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { acid }]} \\
& =4.76+\log \frac{\frac{7.5}{500}}{\frac{5}{500}}=4.7+\log 1.5=4.87
\end{aligned}$
Hence correct answer is $4.76 < \mathrm{pH} < 5.0$

question: Hydrogen ion concentration in mol / L in a solution of pH = 5.4 will be
